ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific Xinjiang-style milk tea entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHV covers tea-based beverages with milk as the closest parent; no Xinjiang-specific code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Xinjiang milk tea or a sufficiently close parent beverage concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot Xinjiang milk tea, approximately 240 g, as typically served at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with whole milk and black tea — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Xinjiang-style milk tea. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) and brewed black tea (FDC ID 1104262), combined in an approximate 60:40 milk-to-tea ratio by weight, consistent with traditional Xinjiang preparation. A small amount of salt (~0.3 g per 100 g beverage) is factored into sodium. This is a component-based estimate; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk and dairy-based beverages — analogous estimate based on whole milk GI (GI=27–34) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Xinjiang milk tea. GI estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk (GI approximately 27–34, mean ~30). The beverage is predominantly milk with brewed tea and salt; the carbohydrate fraction is lactose, which has a low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (30 × 7.68) / 100 ≈ 2.3, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving = 3.2 × 2.4 = 7.68 g.

hypertension.guidance: Xinjiang milk tea is traditionally prepared with added salt, contributing approximately 130 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 312 mg per standard 240 g cup); individuals with hypertension should limit intake or request a low-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by lactose carbohydrate fraction
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.3 per cup, Xinjiang milk tea poses a low glycemic burden and can be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided no sugar is added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk; beneficial tea polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 240 g cup provides approximately 2.9 g saturated fat, which is modest; the black tea component contributes flavonoids with potential cardioprotective effects, making occasional consumption acceptable within a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xinjiang milk tea (新疆奶茶, Xīnjiāng nǎichá) is a traditional hot beverage originating from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of northwestern China, reflecting the Central Asian and Uyghur culinary heritage of the region. It is prepared by boiling brick tea or black tea leaves in water, then combining the strong tea infusion with whole milk or evaporated milk in roughly a 40:60 tea-to-milk ratio, and seasoning with salt rather than sugar — a characteristic that distinguishes it from Cantonese or Taiwanese milk teas. The result is a savoury, creamy, mildly astringent hot drink with a pale amber colour and a smooth, full-bodied texture. In Singapore, it is served at Xinjiang and northwestern Chinese restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant, typically as a hot beverage accompaniment to lamb dishes, hand-pulled noodles, and flatbreads. Nutritionally, a 240 g cup provides approximately 91 kcal, 4.6 g protein, 4.8 g fat, and 7.7 g carbohydrate, primarily as lactose. The sodium content is notably higher than most milk teas due to the added salt, at approximately 312 mg per cup. The glycemic index is low (approximately 30), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The tea polyphenols from black tea may offer mild antioxidant and cardioprotective benefits. Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the salt content.
common_user_queries: Is Xinjiang milk tea sweet or salty?
How many calories in a cup of Xinjiang milk tea?
Is Xinjiang milk tea good for diabetics?
What is nai cha from Xinjiang?
How is Xinjiang milk tea different from bubble tea?
What does 新疆奶茶 taste like?
meal_context: restaurant beverage at northwestern Chinese restaurants

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Xinjiang milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA component data for whole milk and brewed black tea in a traditional preparation ratio. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk. Confidence is medium due to the component-based estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) and brewed black tea (FDC ID 1104262) in an approximate 60:40 milk-to-tea ratio with added salt (~0.3 g/100 g). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA data for the milk and tea components at this preparation level; trace ruminant trans fats in whole milk are below the reporting threshold and rounded to 0.0. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a liquid beverage with no solid plant matter retained after preparation; confirmed zero per USDA brewed tea and milk data. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero; neither whole milk nor brewed black tea contributes meaningful vitamin C. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. 8. GI set to 30 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database whole milk entry, GI ~27–34); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 9. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Beverages', which is the correct classification for a hot milk tea beverage. 10. This food is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay) on field survey data at SGD 6.00 per cup. 11. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Chinese-cuisine item served in Singapore.

Added all mandatory multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi per Singaporean-cuisine rule.
